participants.name: Ravi (q0babf)
participants.professional_persona: Ravi S. is a self-taught AI researcher who co-founded a health-tech startup after dropping out of an NUS PhD programme. His team developed an AI model for early detection of diabetic retinopathy using retinal scans.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Proficient in deep learning for medical imaging, with hands-on experience in GANs for data augmentation. Published in MICCAI despite lacking a formal degree.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To obtain regulatory approval for his diagnostic tool in Singapore and expand pilot deployments to polyclinics across the island.

participants.name: I (yh9dgd)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-yh9dgd9k
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-yh9dgd9k
participants.professional_persona: I am a data scientist at a local bank, and I actively seek partnerships with government agencies to develop AI tools that improve financial literacy for lower-income households. My work focuses on fairness and transparency in credit-scoring models.
participants.skills_and_expertise: I have strong technical skills in Python, machine learning, and data ethics, and I have led two AI projects that were part of the 100 Experiments programme. I also mentor junior analysts on responsible AI practices.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to drive the integration of fairness metrics into all financial AI products in my company. Eventually, I hope to advise national-level bodies on inclusive AI deployment.

participants.name: A (57gc0h)
participants.professional_persona: A former teacher who retrained as an AI practitioner through TeSA's Career Conversion Programme, now working in an edtech startup building personalized learning tools. She blends pedagogical knowledge with AI to create adaptive curricula.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Competent in natural language processing for essay scoring, recommendation systems for learning paths, and educational data mining. Strong at user research and co-designing with students and teachers.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to launch an AI-driven literacy platform for under-resourced schools in Southeast Asia and advocate for inclusive AI in education.

participants.name: A (ezyulw)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-ezyulw9c
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-ezyulw9c
participants.professional_persona: A grassroots volunteer and community organiser in a mature estate, I help run workshops on digital literacy for seniors, showing them how AI-powered tools can make daily tasks easier. I also collect feedback from residents to relay to policymakers.
participants.skills_and_expertise: I have deep community networks and experience in organising outreach events. I also have a basic understanding of common AI applications, such as voice assistants and translation tools, which I demonstrate to participants.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to establish a permanent community hub for digital inclusion in my neighbourhood. My longer-term goal is to become a policy advisor for community engagement in the Smart Nation initiative.

participants.name: Dr. (u7u5yd)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Hwee Ying T. is a Senior Research Manager at AI Singapore, overseeing the 100 Experiments programme and pairing industry partners with academic researchers. She previously led AI teams at Singtel.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Broad knowledge spanning NLP, computer vision, and project management. Skilled in translating business problems into AI research agendas and building cross-sector teams.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To increase the number of successful 100E projects that graduate into commercial products, and to create a playbook for industry-academia AI collaboration.

initial_posts.narrative: As a data scientist who has participated in the 100 Experiments programme, I see the government's commitment of over S$500 million for AI R&D as a solid foundation. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project is clearly stated in the policy and aligns with my experience. For compute infrastructure, I estimate around 500,000 GPU hours based on the need to support multiple concurrent experiments and the typical allocation I've seen for similar initiatives. For trainers, I estimate 50 personnel, drawing from my knowledge of the AI Apprenticeship Programme and the need for hands-on mentorship, though this is a rough guess as the policy doesn't specify exact numbers.

initial_posts.narrative: As Senior Research Manager at AI Singapore, I oversee the 100 Experiments programme and have direct operational knowledge of how we translate our S$500 million budget and 50 trainers into concrete activities. Given the need to source 100 industry problem statements (the programme's target), we typically organize around 40 enterprise outreach sessions—these include sector-specific workshops, industry briefings, and one-on-one consultations—to generate a sufficient pipeline. For researcher-enterprise pairings, we hold about 12 matchmaking events per year, each pairing multiple researchers with several companies, to ensure a good fit and foster collaboration. Training workshops are a key part of our capacity building; with 50 trainers, we can run roughly 20 workshops across different cohorts and topics (e.g., AI fundamentals, deployment best practices) in the 2023 window. Platform onboarding assists are more intensive, as each project requires technical setup on our AI experimentation platform; I estimate about 60 assists, covering initial configuration, data integration, and troubleshooting, based on the typical support needed for the first batch of projects. These numbers align with my experience running the programme and the resource constraints we face.
evidence: As Senior Research Manager at AI Singapore, I directly manage the 100E programme and have firsthand knowledge of the outreach and matchmaking cadence needed to secure 100 problem statements annually.
With 50 trainers available, we can realistically deliver about 20 workshops in a year, given the need for preparation, delivery, and follow-up support for each workshop.

Platform onboarding is a hands-on process requiring dedicated technical assistance; based on past projects, each of the first 60 projects typically needs at least one onboarding session, and some need multiple.

The S$500 million budget and co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project provide the financial runway to support these activities, but the bottleneck is personnel time, which constrains the number of sessions we can run in 2023.

initial_posts.narrative: From my perspective as an AI practitioner who retrained through TeSA and now works in an edtech startup, I see the 100E programme's activities as grounded in the S$500 million budget and 50 trainers. For enterprise outreach, I estimate 35 sessions—fewer than Dr. (u7u5yd)'s 40—because while the policy aims for 100 problem statements, my experience in the startup ecosystem suggests that many enterprises are still hesitant about AI and may require more targeted, smaller-scale engagements rather than large sessions. I base this on my own company's journey: we needed multiple one-on-one consultations before committing to a project. For matchmaking events, I estimate 10, as pairing researchers with enterprises is complex and time-consuming; Dr.'s 12 seems optimistic given the need for careful matching to ensure good fits. Training workshops: I estimate 18, slightly below Dr.'s 20, because with 50 trainers, some will be occupied with other programme activities like outreach and onboarding, reducing workshop capacity. Platform onboarding assists: I estimate 50, lower than Dr.'s 60, because not all projects will require intensive technical setup; many enterprises already have basic digital infrastructure from programmes like SMEs Go Digital. My estimates reflect a practitioner's view of real-world constraints and adoption friction.
evidence: The S$500 million budget and 50 trainers provide a solid base, but my experience in an edtech startup shows that enterprise AI adoption requires extensive hand-holding, so outreach sessions need to be smaller and more personalized to generate quality problem statements.

Matchmaking events are resource-intensive; with only 50 trainers and multiple activities, 10 events per year is a realistic number to ensure meaningful researcher-enterprise pairings, as I've seen in similar initiatives.

Training workshops depend on trainer availability; with 50 trainers also handling outreach and onboarding, 18 workshops is a feasible number, drawing from my retraining journey where workshops were limited by trainer bandwidth.

Platform onboarding assists are critical but not universal; based on my company's experience with AI tools, about half of the projects need intensive support, leading to 50 assists for the first batch of 100E projects.

initial_posts.narrative: As a community engagement officer in Toa Payoh, I see the 100E programme's activities through the lens of grassroots outreach. The S$500 million budget and 50 trainers are impressive, but in my experience, getting enterprises—especially SMEs in mature estates like mine—to engage requires more personalized, smaller-scale efforts. I estimate 30 enterprise outreach sessions, fewer than Dr. (u7u5yd)'s 40 or A (57gc0h)'s 35, because many local businesses are hesitant about AI and need repeated one-on-one consultations rather than large workshops. For researcher-matchmaking events, I estimate 8, as pairing researchers with enterprises is delicate and time-consuming; Dr.'s 12 seems too optimistic given the need for careful matching. Training workshops: I estimate 15, lower than both others, because with 50 trainers, many will be tied up with outreach and onboarding, reducing workshop capacity. Platform onboarding assists: I estimate 40, as not all projects require intensive technical setup; many enterprises already have basic digital infrastructure from programmes like SMEs Go Digital. My estimates reflect a practitioner's view of real-world constraints and adoption friction in community settings.

There was consensus that the AI practitioner pool is approaching but below the 15,000 target (range 12,000–12,500) and that enterprise AI adoption is gradual (range 17.5%–18.5%). initial_posts.narrative: As a health-tech startup founder who has navigated the 100E programme, I see the Impact phase reflecting the cumulative effects of the policy's inputs, activities, outputs, and outcomes. With S$500M funding, 500K GPU hours, and 50 trainers, we generated 80 approved projects, 60 prototypes, 40 trainees, and 50 enterprises with solutions. The outcomes showed 50 deployed projects, a practitioner pool of 12,500, 80 generative AI use cases, and 18% enterprise adoption. These intermediate results position Singapore for improved global AI readiness, but the impact is tempered by real-world friction. I estimate Singapore's global AI readiness rank improves to 6th (from around 7th in 2023), reflecting our strong infrastructure and talent pipeline but still trailing the US, China, UK, Canada, and Germany. The AI sector's GDP share rises to 3.8%, driven by deployed solutions and adoption, though this is below the optimistic 5% some project because many enterprises are still early in their AI journey. Public trust in AI systems reaches 62%, a modest increase from ~55%, as successful deployments in healthcare and government build confidence, but high-profile incidents and job displacement fears keep a significant minority skeptical. AI talent retention is 72%, meaning about 28% of AIAP graduates and other trained talent leave Singapore within a year, drawn by higher salaries abroad or startup opportunities elsewhere. This is a concern but not a crisis, as the policy's ecosystem-building efforts retain the majority. My estimates are grounded in the friction I've seen: enterprises are cautious, deployment takes time, and talent mobility is high. I disagree with any overly optimistic predictions that ignore these realities.
evidence: Singapore's strong compute infrastructure, talent pipeline, and governance framework support a top-10 global AI readiness rank, but competition from the US, China, UK, Canada, and Germany keeps us at 6th, not higher.
The AI sector's GDP share of 3.8% reflects the 50 deployed 100E projects and 80 generative AI use cases, but many enterprises are still in early adoption, limiting the contribution to GDP.

Public trust in AI reaches 62% as successful deployments in healthcare (like our diabetic retinopathy tool) and government services build confidence, but job displacement fears and high-profile incidents keep trust below 70%.

AI talent retention of 72% is realistic because the AIAP and other programmes create strong local networks, but about 28% of graduates leave for higher salaries abroad or startup opportunities, as I've seen among my peers.

AI talent retention is 72%, meaning about 28% of AIAP graduates and other trained talent leave Singapore within a year, drawn by higher salaries abroad or startup opportunities elsewhere. This is a concern but not a crisis, as the policy's ecosystem-building efforts retain the majority. My estimates align closely with Ravi, Dr., and A (ezyulw), and I agree with their cautious realism; I disagree with any overly optimistic predictions that ignore the friction we see daily in enterprise adoption and talent mobility.
